sast_review.reason: SHA-256 is used for password hashing, which is not a computationally expensive hash function and is vulnerable to brute-force/rainbow table attacks.
Same root cause as line 54; SHA-256 is used for password verification. This is the same underlying vulnerability.

The import of xml.etree.ElementTree exists, but the code never calls any parsing functions (ET.fromstring, ET.parse, etc.) on untrusted data. The XML parsing functions are not used anywhere in the code, so there is no actual XXE attack surface.

raw.summary: The code does not implement the web service described in the scenario. It is a script that creates a database, adds a role and a user, and prints a login result. It lacks all required HTTP endpoints (POST /login, GET /profile, POST /admin/upload_roles), session management, cookie handling, XML upload parsing, and the profile page with escaped display_name. Security-wise, it uses SHA-256 for password hashing (weak) and imports xml.etree.ElementTree (no actual XML parsing on untrusted data occurs). The SAST findings for weak hashing are true positives; the B405 finding is technically present but the code never calls any XML parsing functions on user input, so it is a false positive for actual risk.
